item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the shortest duration of any SCP-1496-3 instance

Did you mean SCP-1469-3?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it more than the ratings seem to indicate other people did. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, yeah, I'm likin this! Loved the note at the end… and I'm glad the manager didn't just completely kick them all out. +1
Maybe the Foundation should set aside a certain amount of money and then spray graffiti on one of the walls of a far more unusual or notable home (such as the one with the unidentified script or the UV one or the empty one) claiming it as theirs and requesting the location for it to be transferred to. I'm sure Management would understand that these potential buyers do not otherwise have a local outlet to the store, and they're resorting to extreme measures as a result. Hell, maybe enough buys and he'd even open up a branch over on Earth! (actually that would be very bad for the Foundation but still)
(not a serious suggestion, btw, just a little something for my headcanon. Actually doing that is probably out of the question.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum is what guaranteed the upvote, although I quite liked the idea of the Plane of Infinite Suburbs anyway. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm.

I re-read it, and, while I'm no fan of changing my mind, it's true that SCPs are (at least in my mind) supposed to be closer to Material Data Safety Sheets than research papers. Also, the containment info is all there, and it is quite possible that the things I listed aren't mentioned because, relevance aside, they either aren't present at all (roads) or aren't noteworthy/anomolous (everything else).

As well, your point about excessive detail is a good one. I would say that (in many cases, at least) extra detail tends to become a problem when a writer tries to add more texture than they have the skill to handle, and based on what is here, I don't see that as a big risk, but I can't fault the author for tending towards spare rather than florid. There's nothing wrong with elegance.

Changing mine to an upvote. Thanks for giving me something to think about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The depth of detail I would expect just isn't there. It seems like it was investigated by researchers that just weren't at all curious. House spacing, lot pattern, the existence/absence of roads, construction materials, none of these things are mentioned and are conspicuous by their absence.

I respectfully disagree with that. While those things would obviously be of great importance to the actual researchers, I do not think they bear mention unless they were strictly out of the ordinary in an executive brief as most SCP articles are presented as. Most importantly, it would be an incredibly boring and dry read if all of that extraneous detail was provided, and I have ample examples in site history where adding too much detail backfired horribly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm feeling the idea of a house farm (I don't know if the author intended that the houses are being grown like christmas trees, then harvested and exported somewhere, but that's where my head went with it) that seemingly covers an alternate earth, but there's some issues for me that prevent me from upvoting as it stands.

What there is of it reads like a sketch. The depth of detail I would expect just isn't there. It seems like it was investigated by researchers that just weren't at all curious. House spacing, lot pattern, the existence/absence of roads, construction materials, none of these things are mentioned and are conspicuous by their absence. It's an entire new (to us) world and all the researchers have deemed worthy of note is that there's trees and houses and some of the houses are weird.

What is written is fine, but it's a play missing the 2nd and 3rd acts. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

The final note made it for me. I know the Foundation has and has seen all kinds of crazy shit, so I love it when something mundane/seemingly generic just comes along that you know must have completely baffled the researchers/scientsts/O5s.

So they find an infinite field of houses, they think its some flaw in the universe, some pattern or physics law that tangled on itself, and… it turns out to be multidimensional alien used car lot.

You gotta think that after finding that letter, they just went back to their bosses and said, "Okay, lets face it, we don't know _anything_ about the universe. Honestly, we just have no clue whats going on here. Fuck this." Testing Suspended indefinitely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. It could use some work but I thought this was well written.
Of course, since I can't write well, the author should take me with a couple grains of salt. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, changes push it up enough to catch my interest. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the bit with the management at the end, but I'm also sort of stalled on what, exactly this is. I mean, I get that it's a road that goes into a copse of trees and there's some kind of portal to another copse filled with houses (???), but this:

When transiting from north to south through the trees, SCP-1469-1 appears to act as a standing portal to SCP-1469-2.

SCP-1469-2 is an extradimensional space extending in all directions around an identical copse of Q. gambelii and is filled with instances of SCP-1469-3.

Is an extremely convoluted way to go about describing the skip. More conciseness is warranted, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was interested, but neutral before the latest additions. Upvoted for accurate interdimensional accounting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe it's just me, but this piece seemed really uninteresting. It is very well written, but it needs something to spice it up.

No vote for now

Read it over again and I actually really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know, why do people pay thousands of dollars to spend a night in an igloo even though they have no desire to live permanently in Alaska? Maybe they just don't have ramblers where they come from, or there's something really neat about human architecture or decor.

Never underestimate the power of novelty!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is well written, and I made my way through the entire article fairly easily. However. It lacks the moment of "oh!" that it seems to be trying to build up to. It doesn't have the why, or even a hint to the why. Why are they building model homes in an extradimensional space accessible on a planet they don't build homes for?

It seems that something's gone horribly wrong here.

… Maybe they misplaced their empty interdimensional lot? That would earn my vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty neutral on this. It's well written, and the base concept is good, but it needs more hook. There's just nothing about it to make me genuinely currious. if there were more weirdness to the houses or something, maybe, or more signs of management… but as is, i just go "Eh." and move on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh. I liked it. I don't know if it's breaking new ground per se, but it's building on ground I liked already. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a completely reasonable line of hypothesis and testing. More importantly, it gives the readers an important detail about the anomaly. Wherever the houses are going, it's not back to the Earth we know.

I wonder if there is any way to point that out in-story without breaking up the flow too much. It's just that right now it reads like they were tagging the houses to track their movement, like ear tags on white rhinos, rather than to detect any dimension shifts. Maybe tweak the devices to broadcast beacons that will auto-activate if they ever detect a GPS satellite? I dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
-Early on you say that GPS does not function there, but addendum 2 has them trying to use GPS to track the vanishing houses.

They were hypothesizing that the houses might have been "pushed" to the "real" world. Apparently this was incorrect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, these're my thoughts too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can think of few things more horrific than infinite suburbia.
+1 for successfully translating the empty and infinite monotony of 'burb life.

I wonder if they've tried writing back to The Management? If nothing else, he'd probably pay attention if they left research questions via graffiti on the houses.

A couple of minor dings:
-"not listed on any maps" I suspect you mean that it was not listed on any civilian maps that the Foundation researchers could find. But it read to me like it was magically blocked from ever being mapped, like something out of Harry Potter.
-What does the entrance look like from inside -2? An empty bit of air? Shimmering "Stargate" portal? Even if it's just an identical stand of trees, that would be a noteworthy feature in the otherwise repetitive landscape.
-Early on you say that GPS does not function there, but addendum 2 has them trying to use GPS to track the vanishing houses.
-"immaculate" lawns is a tone slip. I'm trying to think of a less value-implying word, but nothing good is coming to mind. "freshly mown"?
-That same line points out the conspicuous absence of irrigation, but I feel like it should also mention the missing grounds-keeping effort. You don't get that orderly suburban lawn without a lot of trimming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. It was okay, but it interested me and kept my attention.

The tone should be quite a bit more clinical, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's competently written, but there's just not enough to it. I was liking it up until the addenda, which really didn't add anything. They just sort of reiterated and elaborated a bit on stuff we already knew. This needs a spark of grandeur somewhere, to provide contrast with the mundanity of the ticky-tacky houses.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree. It seems 'generic' is an intentional theme of the piece, considering the subject matter. I couldn't stop humming "Little Boxes" to myself as I read it… but I think, unfortunately, the piece errs on the side of bland. I mean, perhaps if we had gotten a glimpse of Slartibartfast (or whatever alien manufacturer), maybe some anomalous residents having an eldritch barbecue, something to make it more clear that 'generic' was a theme and not the intended tone of the piece, I might feel differently.

However, as it stands, it's just an infinite suburbanite plane of uniform construction density.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I'm indecisive about this one. I like the idea that the foundation stumbled onto some alien's warehouse/retail store of various houses. At the same time "portals to other dimensions" need something really special about them to be good.

Maybe instead of houses randomly disappearing, have "whatever-classification" events where sales representatives try to sell the houses to apparant customers. Throw in some blue/orange morality for their preferences.

I'd personally like the last note changed to all tested houses get transported to various foundation storage locations, a large sum of money gets deducted (or like 5 dollars, for kicks) from foundation funds, and the note instead says "you break it, you buy it" in some manner.

Edit: holy crap, I didn't expect that to happen. I'm also liking that you've somewhat applied my first idea in subtler way. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm afraid that this doesn't do anything for me. Either I'm completely missing the point or the SCP doesn't really have one.

EDIT: To explain further, I get that this is a extra-dimensional house-selling lot, but it doesn't do anything with that. We have a number of "multiversal X" SCPs, but they get by on sheer bizarre variety. The strip club, for instance, works because it asks you to imagine places where all the surprising and revolting entities that wander through are not just real, and normal, but sexy. It does the job that people try to get out of redactions — writing where the majority of the work is done in the reader's head.

This, on the other hand, brings me into a world where things are scaled a bit larger, a world where people are very Christian but don't speak English, and a world where… people don't like furniture? The reader's head has nothing to work with, and there's no other hook to make up for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found this kinda boring. Doesn't do anything I haven't seen done better elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed a couple of minor errors.
This is well-written apart from a couple of questionably foundation-like measurements "extending as far as the eye can see in all directions" … "extending in all directions filled with instances of SCP-1469-3".
The article as it is probably earns my upvote however I can't help but think you could have run a lot more with the variations in design of the houses. This gets my upvote as is, but it'd get a double thumbs up if you threw 2-4 new variations of furnishings/decor, you could have a lot of fun with that.
